1: Hezekiah sends invitations throughout all Israel and Judah, including letters to Ephraim and Manasseh. Writing to the northern tribes — by this point largely under Assyrian occupation after Tiglath-pileser III’s campaigns (734-732 BCE) and Sargon II’s final deportation (722 BCE) — is unprecedented. The Chronicler presents Hezekiah as the king who refuses to accept the political division of Israel as the final word on the covenant community’s unity.
2: The second-month Passover is grounded in Num 9:10-11: those who are unclean or on a journey may observe Passover in the second month on the fourteenth day. Hezekiah invokes this legitimate provision because of two specific impediments: insufficient priestly consecration and the people not yet assembled (v3). The Chronicler demonstrates that Hezekiah’s accommodation is not arbitrary creativity but careful application of Torah provision.
3: The two stated reasons for the delay — insufficient priestly consecration and people not assembled — are both temporary, curable conditions. The implication is that the remedy was underway: priests were consecrating themselves (ch 29:34 notes their eventual sufficiency) and the assembly was being gathered. MKT renders “not enough priests had consecrated themselves” rather than a stronger reading, to preserve the factual rather than fault-ascribing tone of the verse.
4: The plan “seemed right” (wayyiššar haddāḇār) to the king and the assembly — the idiom of legitimate corporate decision-making (cf. 1 Chr 13:2; Acts 15:28: “it seemed right to the Holy Spirit and to us”). The decision is not unilateral royal decree but consensus of king and assembly, mirroring the covenant-renewal assemblies throughout Chronicles.
5: The decree goes out “from Beersheba to Dan” — the traditional formulation for all Israel from south to north (cf. Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 3:20; 2 Sam 3:10). The breadth of the invitation is the key point: this Passover is not a regional Jerusalem celebration but a summons to the whole covenant people regardless of the political border that now separates Israel from Judah. “For it had not been celebrated in great numbers as prescribed” — acknowledging a long period of covenant neglect.
6: The letter’s opening: “People of Israel, return (šûḇû) to the LORD, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel.” The call to return (šûḇ — the covenant-return verb) invokes the patriarchal God by his full triple name — the name that encompasses the whole Abrahamic promise. The use of “Israel” (Jacob’s covenant name) rather than “Jacob” as the third patriarch is deliberate: the northern tribes share a claim on the patriarch Israel and are being called back to that identity.
7: “Do not be like your fathers and brothers, who were unfaithful (māʿalû).” The letter uses māʿal (covenant faithlessness) — the same verb Hezekiah used of his own fathers in 29:6. The identification of the northern tribes’ sin with Judah’s own history of apostasy is irenic: Hezekiah does not condemn Israel as uniquely apostate but places them in a shared history of failure that includes Judah. The result — “he made them a horror (lĕšammâ)” — recycles the 29:8 language.
8: “Do not be stiff-necked (qĕšê ʿōrep) like your ancestors.” The idiom qāšâ ʿōrep (hard of neck) is the Deuteronomic term for covenant resistance (Exod 32:9; 33:3, 5; Deut 9:6, 13; 10:16). The neck metaphor: an animal that refuses to yield to the yoke is “stiff-necked.” The appeal: “submit to the LORD (tĕnû yād laYHWH) — literally ‘give a hand to the LORD’ — the gesture of submission and covenant entry (cf. Lam 5:6). “Come to his sanctuary which he has consecrated forever (lĕʿôlām)” — the sanctuary’s permanent divine dedication makes it always available for returning worshippers.
9: “For if you return (tāšûḇû) to the LORD, your brothers and children will find compassion (rāḥămîm) from their captors.” The covenant motivation for return is practical: the exiled northern Israelites may be released if the remnant returns to YHWH. The ground: “The LORD your God is gracious and merciful (ḥannûn wĕraḥûm).” This is the Sinai covenant-name formula (Exod 34:6: ḥannûn wĕraḥûm ʾerek ʾappayim wĕrab ḥesed wĕʾĕmet), which grounds every prophetic call to return in the character of the God being addressed.
10: The couriers go through Ephraim, Manasseh, and Zebulun — “but the people laughed and mocked them (wayyiśḥăqû wĕyĕlallĕgû ʿălêhem).” The verb śāḥaq (laugh/mock) is the word for Sarah’s disbelieving laughter at the promise (Gen 18:12-13) and for Ishmael’s mocking of Isaac (Gen 21:9). The double verb (śāḥaq + lāʿag) indicates contemptuous derision — the invitation to covenant restoration is met with ridicule. This is the expected response from those whose apostasy is entrenched.
11: “Nevertheless, some men from Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun humbled themselves (kānĕʿû) and came to Jerusalem.” The remnant who respond are specifically those who “humbled themselves” — kānaʿ (humble/submit/be brought low) is the Chronicler’s signature verb for the repentance that averts divine judgment (cf. 2 Chr 7:14; 12:6-7; 33:12, 19, 23). The specific tribes — Asher, Manasseh, Zebulun — all from the north and west — show the response was geographically distributed, not concentrated.
12: “In Judah too God’s hand gave them one heart (yad hāʾĕlōhîm hāytâ bihûdâ lātēt lāhem lēḇ ʾeḥād).” The divine sovereign gift of unified hearts is the precondition for covenant assembly. The phrase lēḇ ʾeḥād (one heart) appears in 1 Chr 12:38 for the all-Israel gathering at Hebron to make David king. Heart-unity is the gift of God, not the achievement of royal diplomacy — the same theology of Ezek 11:19; 36:26 (“I will give them one heart”).
13: “A large crowd — a very great assembly (qāhāl rāb mĕʾōd).” The characterization “very great assembly” is the maximum superlative of communal gathering in Chronicles — the same language used for Solomon’s dedication assembly (1 Kgs 8:65) and the eschatological assembly of Rev 7:9. The Festival of Unleavened Bread in the second month marks the occasion: the seven-day feast that follows Passover extends the celebration and the communal gathering.
14: They remove the foreign altars and incense altars from Jerusalem and throw them into the Kidron Brook. The spontaneous cult-reform by the gathered assembly — not commanded by Hezekiah — indicates that genuine repentance is occurring: the people themselves act to purify the city before the feast. The Kidron Brook continues to be the disposal channel for cult contaminants (cf. 2 Chr 15:16; 29:16).
15: “The priests and Levites were ashamed (wayyēḇōšû).” The shame of the priests is a striking detail: they had not adequately consecrated themselves, but the sight of the people’s spontaneous reform shamed them into action. The verb bôš (be ashamed/put to shame) is elsewhere used for the shame of those whose trust was misplaced (Ps 25:3; 31:1, 17; Isa 49:23). Here it is productive shame — the kind that motivates right action rather than paralysis.
16: The Levites receive the Passover blood and hand it to the priests, who sprinkle it — “as prescribed, following the law of Moses the man of God.” The explicit appeal to Mosaic law underlines Hezekiah’s reform’s legitimacy. “Moses the man of God” (ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm) is Moses’s most honorific title in the Chronicler’s vocabulary (cf. 1 Chr 23:14; Ezra 3:2; Neh 12:24) — invoking this title at the moment of describing Torah compliance signals maximum covenantal gravity.
17: The Levites take over the slaughtering for those not ritually clean — a practical accommodation driven by the extraordinary circumstances. Numbers 9:10-11 authorized the second-month Passover for those ritually impure; the Levites extend the accommodation further by serving as proxy slaughterers for those who cannot themselves fulfil the requirement. The Chronicler presents this as legitimate expansion of Mosaic accommodation rather than violation — necessity and divine provision rather than disregard of the law.
18: “A large number of the people... had not purified themselves, yet they ate the Passover contrary to what was written.” The Chronicler does not soften the admission: the second-month Passover was eaten by many who were still ritually impure — an objective violation of Lev 7:20-21. The solution is not ritual but intercessory: “But Hezekiah prayed for them: ‘May the good LORD pardon (yĕkappēr) everyone.’” The verb kāpar (atone/cover) appears — Hezekiah offers an intercessory prayer of atonement for those whose heart is right but whose ritual status is wrong.
19: “Who has set his heart to seek God (lā​dĕrōš lēʾlōhîm), the LORD, the God of his ancestors, even if he is not cleansed as the sanctuary requires.” This is the theological centrepiece of the chapter — and possibly of all Chronicles’ teaching on worship. Hezekiah’s prayer establishes a priority: seeking-heart (dāraš) trumps ritual cleanness. The intention is decisive, not the technical status. This does not abolish the ritual requirements but establishes that God responds to sincere seeking even when full compliance is temporarily impossible — a principle with direct New Testament resonance (John 4:23-24).
20: “The LORD listened to Hezekiah and healed (wayyirpāʾ) the people.” The verb rāpāʾ (heal) is unexpected — the people were ritually impure, not physically sick. But the Chronicler uses healing language for YHWH’s response to Hezekiah’s intercession, treating ritual impurity as a kind of “sickness” of the community that divine mercy can address. The divine response is direct, immediate, and total — he “healed” them, removing the impurity’s consequence through intercessory prayer.
21: Seven days of the Festival of Unleavened Bread with “great joy” (śimḥâ gĕdôlâ). “The Levites and priests praised the LORD every day with resounding instruments.” The word “resounding” (kĕlê ʿōz laYHWH — “instruments of strength/force for the LORD”) captures the intensity and power of the praise. Seven days of continuous daily praise — the same seven-day structure as the Feast of Booths (Lev 23:36) and Solomon’s dedication feast (2 Chr 7:8-9).
22: Hezekiah “spoke encouragingly (wĕdibber ʿal lēḇ) to all the Levites who showed skill and understanding in the LORD’s service.” The idiom dibbēr ʿal lēḇ — “speak to the heart” — is the language of intimate, reassuring address: Boaz to Ruth (Ruth 2:13), Joseph to his brothers (Gen 50:21), YHWH to Jerusalem (Isa 40:2). Hezekiah’s personal encouragement of the Levites reflects the king’s role as patron and sustainer of Levitical worship.
23: “Then the whole assembly decided to celebrate for another seven days, and they did so with great joy.” The spontaneous extension of the feast for a second week is the congregation’s own initiative — not a royal decree. The Chronicler underlines this: “the whole assembly decided (wayyiwwāʿăṣû)” — they took counsel and resolved. The assembly acts with covenantal agency, not merely royal compliance. The total fourteen-day feast matches Solomon’s dedication feast (2 Chr 7:8-9: seven days + seven days = fourteen days).
24: The royal and official contributions: Hezekiah gives 1,000 bulls and 7,000 sheep; officials give 1,000 bulls and 10,000 sheep. The scale of royal generosity triggers priestly consecration: “a great number of priests consecrated themselves.” The connection is explicit — the abundance of sacrificial material creates the occasion and perhaps the incentive for delayed priestly consecrations. Covenant renewal creates its own momentum.
25: The composition of the full assembly is described: all Judah, the priests, the Levites, those who came from Israel, the “foreigners (gērîm) who had come from Israel,” and those who lived in Judah. The inclusion of gērîm (resident aliens) who came from northern Israel is significant — non-ethnic Israelites are full participants in the Passover, consistent with the Mosaic provision (Num 9:14; Exod 12:48-49).
26: “There was great joy in Jerusalem — nothing like it had been seen since the time of Solomon son of David king of Israel.” The explicit Solomon comparison is the Chronicler’s highest possible praise for a post-Solomonic assembly. Solomon’s dedication feast (2 Chr 7:8-10) is the benchmark; Hezekiah’s Passover matches it in scale and joy. The phrase “son of David king of Israel” — not “king of Judah” — reminds the reader that Solomon was king of all Israel, and Hezekiah’s all-Israel Passover is reaching for that same unity.
27: “The priests and Levites stood up to bless the people (lĕḇārēk). Their voice was heard, and their prayer ascended (wayyāḇōʾ) to his holy dwelling place — to heaven (lĕmāʿôn qodĕšô laššāmāyim).” The climactic verse of the chapter: the priestly blessing (Num 6:24-26) followed by the ascent of the prayer to the heavenly sanctuary. The phrase mĕʿôn qodĕš (holy habitation) is used of the heavenly temple in Ps 68:5; Deut 26:15; Isa 63:15 — YHWH’s true dwelling is not the Jerusalem temple but heaven itself, of which the temple is the earthly counterpart.
